proposalState.problemStatement: Students understand sorting algorithm names but cannot trace execution on a given array. SortQuest makes sorting visual and tactile: drag tiles into correct positions following real algorithm rules. Wrong individual moves are rejected immediately with an algorithm-specific explanation — there is no path to a correct final state through random dragging.

sixDim.what: 8 puzzle modes (Sequential Sort, Binary Search, Insertion Sort, MergeSort, QuickSort, HeapSort, DP Knapsack, BFS Graph); drag-and-drop tile engine with algorithm-rule validation; star rating (★★★ = min moves + under par time); hint system (−1 star); Daily Sort Challenge (global seed, leaderboard); Level Editor (shareable URL)
sixDim.why: Flow: 4 tiles (level 1) → 64 tiles (level 5). SDT Competence: visible improvement. MDA: Mechanics = constrained drag-drop; Dynamics = optimal move discovery; Aesthetic = Mastery + Sensation (satisfying merge animation).
sixDim.who: Free Spirits 40% (experiment, try to break algorithm), Achievers 40% (3-star perfection, Daily leaderboard), Explorers 20% (edge cases in QuickSort pivot)
sixDim.when: Modes unlock sequentially with chapters. Daily Challenge always available. Level Editor from week 2.
sixDim.how: Wrong moves rejected immediately with algorithm-specific explanation ("This violates MergeSort rule: left pointer must advance monotonically"). No ambiguity. 0 false accepts by validation function.
sixDim.where: CSS Grid + HTML5 Drag-and-Drop API, single-file browser app. Level Editor encodes puzzle as shareable URL.

proposalState.narrativeTheme: The SortQuest realm is in chaos — the Library of Ordered Knowledge has been scrambled by the Entropy Demon. As the last Algorithm Keeper, restore order using the ancient sorting laws.
proposalState.characters:
Algorithm Engineer (Student): Selects and deploys correct algorithms to clear each complexity wave using knowledge and pattern recognition.
Complexity Horde (NPC Enemy): Waves of algorithmic problems with specific complexity classes requiring matched algorithm types.
Instructor Avatar (NPC Guide): In-game professor providing contextual hints, chapter intros, and debrief prompts.
proposalState.storyWorld: The Sorting Academy is an ancient institution where computational knowledge is tested not by memorisation but by execution. Six halls represent the six sorting algorithm families: the Bubble Hall, the Selection Chamber, the Insertion Corridor, the Merge Sanctum, the Quick Arena, and the Heap Pinnacle. Each hall tests a specific algorithm family with increasing input complexity and edge-case difficulty. The Academy Boss Puzzle chamber sits at the pinnacle — accessible only to students who have demonstrated mastery across all six halls.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Validation function rejects any move violating algorithm rules
conceptMappings.dynamic: MergeSort: cannot skip merge step; left pointer must advance monotonically
conceptMappings.rationale: Constraint-enforcement turns the puzzle from tile-sort into algorithm-execution: student is constrained to think and act like the algorithm itself

conceptMappings.mechanic: Move counter; star rating penalises excess moves
conceptMappings.dynamic: BubbleSort min 28 moves on 8 tiles; 40 moves = star drops
conceptMappings.rationale: Move counter makes efficiency visceral — fewer moves feels better, which is the intuition behind optimisation

conceptMappings.mechanic: Tap tile to declare pivot; board auto-colours red (>) blue (<); partition follows
conceptMappings.dynamic: Worst-case pivot (always max) → maximally unbalanced → more moves → star drop
conceptMappings.rationale: Visual partition immediately shows why random/median-of-3 strategies outperform worst-case picks

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Puzzle chain (sequential sorting challenges), Algorithm selection panel, Step-by-step execution validation, Hint token system (3 per session), Sandbox mode (unscored experimentation), Leaderboard by difficulty level reached, Speed-sort challenge mode.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: The step-by-step execution validation mechanic forces students to think at the algorithm's execution level rather than just knowing the algorithm name, producing deeper cognitive engagement. Hint token scarcity creates authentic cost-benefit reasoning about knowledge confidence. Sandbox mode provides a low-stakes exploration space that complements the scored puzzle chain.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Challenge (each puzzle requires genuine algorithm application, not guessing), Discovery (sandbox mode enables free algorithm experimentation), Accomplishment (advanced difficulty puzzle completion signals genuine SEN4012 mastery), Submission (puzzle aesthetic creates engaging spatial representation of abstract sorting concepts).

proposalState.introText: Welcome to the Sorting Academy. You are an algorithm apprentice, and your task is simple: sort the data correctly, step by step, using the algorithm the puzzle demands. Choose the wrong algorithm and the step-level validator will show exactly where your execution diverged from the correct trace — no vague wrong answers here. Use your hint tokens wisely; you have only three per session. Complete each sorting hall to advance and reach the Boss Puzzle chamber where the full sorting hierarchy is tested in a single complex trial. The Academy is waiting.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct full platform test on target lab devices 24h before session. Prepare a paper-based fallback version of the sorting puzzle game challenges. Verify browser compatibility on all student devices.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Switch to prepared paper-based fallback immediately. Instructor verbally presents SEN4012 algorithm challenges; students answer on printed worksheets. Session data manually entered into system post-session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Widespread Rule Misunderstanding at Session Start
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Provide multi-modal game introduction: verbal briefing, printed rule card, and in-game tutorial overlay for the sorting puzzle game. Conduct a 2-minute practice round before official scoring begins.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator pauses game immediately on detecting widespread confusion. Re-delivers rule explanation using a specific worked example. Q&A window opened before resuming. Game timer reset if necessary.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design sorting puzzle game scoring to reward both performance and SEN4012 algorithm concept demonstration. Integrate mandatory reflection checkpoints mid-session where students must explain their decision rationale.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor intervenes to redirect student focus to SEN4012 algorithm learning objectives. Post-session assessment weights conceptual understanding over game performance score explicitly.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Implement adaptive difficulty that reduces challenge intensity if a student is failing repeatedly in the sorting puzzle game. Provide visible progress indicators so students always see incremental advancement.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator provides individual encouragement and targeted hint. Adaptive system reduces challenge difficulty for the next SEN4012 algorithm task. Optional easier entry point offered without public announcement.

tuslerRisks.risk: Assessment Misalignment with Course Learning Outcomes
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct formal SEN4012 algorithm curriculum alignment review before deployment. Map every sorting puzzle game challenge item to specific course learning outcome statements in the alignment matrix.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor conducts post-session targeted debrief correcting any SEN4012 algorithm misconceptions surfaced by game challenge outcomes. Misaligned challenge items flagged for removal before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Incorrect Big-O Reasoning Reinforced as Correct
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Embed automated Big-O correctness checking in the sorting puzzle game — system must validate algorithm classification against verified answer key before awarding XP. Manual review of edge cases by instructor before deployment.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor delivers targeted post-session debrief correcting identified Big-O misconceptions. Specific algorithm challenge items producing widespread incorrect answers flagged for redesign before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Step-Level Validation Feels Punitive Rather Than Educational
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design validation feedback as positive guidance rather than binary fail: 'This step is valid but consider whether this is the most efficient next move.' Animations reinforce correct steps before flagging errors.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor reframes error messages in session debrief as diagnostic information, not punishment. Post-session error pattern analysis shared with students as a personalised learning roadmap for next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Sandbox Mode Time Consumption Leaving Insufficient Session Time
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Enforce a maximum sandbox time allocation of 8 minutes per session, with a visible countdown and automatic transition to scored puzzle chain when the allocation expires. Sandbox findings prompts students to apply to scored chain.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator announces sandbox time limit 2 minutes before expiry. Students who have not transitioned receive a facilitator-directed starter puzzle aligned to their sandbox exploration to bridge the transition.

tuslerRisks.risk: Boss Puzzle Algorithm Selection Produces Single Correct Answer Perception
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design Boss Puzzle with two demonstrably valid algorithm solutions at different complexity-trade-off points. Award full XP for both, but differentiate feedback to highlight the performance difference between the two correct approaches.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor uses Boss Puzzle multiple-valid-solution structure in debrief to teach algorithm selection reasoning beyond binary correctness — reinforcing that SEN4012 algorithm selection is context-dependent, not prescriptive.

proposalState.gameSubject: Sorting algorithms: bubble, insertion, merge, quick, heap, counting — Levitin Chapters 3-7

proposalState.gameOverview: A tile-puzzle visualiser for SEN4012 where students physically sort array tiles using a chosen algorithm — each move costs XP proportional to its operation count.
proposalState.deliverableJust: A browser-based tile puzzle is uniquely suited because: (1) Active decision-making with immediate consequence creates stronger encoding than passive delivery; (2) Game mechanics directly parallel domain processes (see Concept-to-Game Mapping); (3) Performance logging enables objective Bloom L3+ skill assessment.
proposalState.sdtAutonomy: Students choose their approach to each sorting puzzle independently — selecting which sorting algorithm to apply, determining step sequence, and deciding when to use hint tokens versus reasoning independently. The open sandbox mode allows students to freely experiment with algorithm variants without scoring pressure, supporting self-directed algorithm exploration beyond the structured puzzle chain.
proposalState.sdtCompetence: Each puzzle completion with a correct sorting algorithm application awards XP proportional to the solution's time complexity optimality, making algorithm knowledge directly rewarding in a quantifiable way. The puzzle difficulty ladder (Bubble Sort fundamentals to Heap Sort) provides an explicit, topic-aligned competence staircase. Incorrect solutions reveal the execution step where the chosen algorithm diverged from correct behaviour.
proposalState.sdtRelatedness: Post-session puzzle board shared with the class shows which students reached which difficulty levels, creating peer conversation about sorting algorithm trade-offs. Group speed-sort challenges (simultaneous puzzle solving with live class comparison) create shared competitive energy. Pair mode allows two students to collaboratively solve a sorting challenge, requiring negotiated algorithm selection.
proposalState.platform: Browser-based web application; any modern browser. No installation required. Compatible with IKU computer labs, student devices, and mobile browsers.
proposalState.narrativeEvolution:
Hall 1-2 — Bubble and Selection Halls (O(n²) Fundamentals): Apprentice completes Bubble Sort and Selection Sort puzzles at increasing input sizes. Step-level validation reveals common O(n²) execution misconceptions early. Hint tokens tempting but advised to save. SEN4012 concept: O(n²) sorting algorithm execution patterns and comparison count analysis.

Hall 3-4 — Insertion and Merge Halls (Transition to O(n log n)): Insertion Sort puzzles test best-case awareness. Merge Sort introduces divide-and-conquer execution tracing — the first multi-step recursive structure. Complexity jumps; some students use first hint tokens here. SEN4012 concept: insertion sort best-case O(n) and merge sort O(n log n) divide-and-conquer execution.

Hall 5-6 — QuickSort and Heap Halls (O(n log n) Mastery): Pivot selection in QuickSort puzzles tests worst-case awareness. HeapSort introduces heap property validation as a pre-sort step. Advanced students preserve hint tokens; others spend them here. SEN4012 concept: QuickSort pivot strategy, worst-case O(n²) scenario, and HeapSort O(n log n) guarantee.

Boss Puzzle — Mixed-Requirement Trial: The Academy presents a complex scenario with multiple data sets, each requiring a different optimal sorting algorithm. Correct algorithm selection for each data set determines the final quality score. Debrief maps every step-level decision to SEN4012 sorting algorithm selection criteria and comparative analysis.
proposalState.logPrivacy: Activity logs retained 6 months. Accessible only to facilitator and research team. Student identifiers anonymised in aggregate reports. Data per KVKK and GDPR. Students informed via course syllabus. Data destruction certificate at academic year end.
proposalState.howEthics: Student participation is voluntary; equivalent written task available for opt-outs. All performance data anonymised in aggregate reports. Data collected under KVKK and GDPR, disclosed in course syllabus. Assessment based on participation and reflection, not competitive ranking. Instructor monitors emotional wellbeing; distress triggers private check-in.
proposalState.whyMotivation: Students are motivated through three SDT core drives: Autonomy — puzzle approach selection and sandbox exploration give students genuine control over their algorithm learning strategy, respecting that different students prefer different entry points into algorithm understanding. Competence — step-by-step execution validation provides far more precise feedback than answer-level checking, making algorithm understanding feel like a real, verifiable skill rather than an arbitrary test performance. Relatedness — group speed-sort challenges and pair mode transform individual algorithm puzzle-solving into shared class events with authentic peer engagement. Through the Octalysis lens: Challenge, Development & Accomplishment, Discovery, Social Influence.

gameDynamics.progressionSystems: Puzzle chain advances through six sorting algorithm families in complexity order: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Merge Sort, QuickSort, and Heap Sort. Each family contains 3 puzzles of increasing input size and edge-case complexity. Completing a family unlocks the next and awards a family badge. The final boss puzzle requires choosing and applying the optimal algorithm for a complex mixed-requirement scenario.
gameDynamics.feedbackLoops: Step-validation feedback highlights the exact execution step where a student's algorithm trace diverges from correct behaviour, providing precise actionable feedback. Hint token use is tracked and displayed in the post-session report, providing feedback on knowledge confidence patterns. Sandbox mode provides self-directed feedback through observable algorithm execution without scoring pressure.
gameDynamics.competition: Leaderboard by puzzle chain depth and efficiency (hints used per puzzle). Weekly Sort Master badge for the student with the deepest chain and fewest hints used. Speed-sort challenge mode (all students attempt the same puzzle simultaneously with live class timer) creates high-energy competitive events throughout the semester.
gameDynamics.collaboration: Pair mode pairs students on a shared puzzle: one selects the algorithm, one executes the steps. Role alternation per puzzle ensures both students engage both selection and execution competencies. Disagreements on algorithm selection trigger a collaborative reasoning phase before commitment to the solution path.
gameDynamics.adaptability: Puzzle difficulty and input complexity are configurable per session. Hint token allocation is adjustable for assessment vs practice contexts. Sandbox mode can be enabled or disabled per session. Boss puzzle can be swapped per session to cover different algorithm category combinations. Speed-sort challenge timing is adjustable.

proposalState.personaList:
Persona 1 — The Precision Solver: Approaches each puzzle with full algorithm analysis before beginning. Rarely uses hints. Motivated by completing the boss puzzle with zero hints.

Persona 2 — The Sandbox Explorer: Prefers unscored sandbox mode for algorithm experimentation before attempting scored puzzles. Uses sandbox to test incorrect approaches safely before committing.

Persona 3 — The Speed Runner: Prioritises puzzle completion speed over optimality. May select a correct but suboptimal sorting algorithm for large inputs to avoid decision time cost. Needs instructor prompts to reconsider efficiency.

realizations.label: Problem Statement
realizations.hint: ≥80 chars + a percentage for full credit
realizations.promised: Students understand sorting algorithm names but cannot trace execution on a given array. SortQuest makes sorting visual and tactile: drag tiles into correct positions following real algorithm rules. Wrong individual moves are rejected immediately with an algorithm-specific explanation — there is no path to a correct final state through random dragging.
realizations.proof: The problem is addressed by the deployed SortQuest prototype. Students can practise algorithm execution through tile-based puzzle interactions. Invalid moves are rejected with algorithm-specific feedback, helping players understand why a step does not follow the selected algorithm rule.

realizations.label: Target Population
realizations.promised: SEN4012 Analysis of Algorithms students (3rd/4th year, n≈35)
realizations.proof: The game is designed for SEN4012 Analysis of Algorithms students. The selected modes and puzzle content focus on sorting, searching, heap operations, and graph traversal topics that are relevant to the course.

realizations.label: Success Criteria
realizations.proof: Formal success criteria were not fully defined in the imported proposal. However, the deployed game supports measurable indicators such as completion rate, score, moves, time, hints used, stars, and leaderboard entries. These can be used during the play phase to evaluate participation and playability.

realizations.label: User Stories

realizations.proof: Most user stories are implemented in the deployed prototype. Students can play tile-based algorithm puzzles, receive feedback for invalid moves, see final score and star rating, use hints, create a custom puzzle with the Level Editor, and generate a shareable URL. The Daily Challenge and leaderboard are implemented as browser-based local prototypes, not as a global backend leaderboard.

realizations.proof: The main functional requirements are implemented: CSS grid tile board, tile interaction, algorithm-specific validation, 8 puzzle modes, score and star rating, hint system, Level Editor, and shareable URL generation. The Daily Challenge exists in the prototype, but the leaderboard is stored locally in the browser instead of using a global online backend.

realizations.label: Non-Functional Requirements

realizations.proof: The game provides responsive browser-based interaction, immediate visual feedback, and invalid move rejection for all modes. However, the full automated validation test suite with 200 invalid scenarios per mode was not implemented. Therefore, the usability goals are mostly addressed, while the complete automated NFR verification remains future work.

realizations.label: Log Privacy Statement
realizations.promised: Activity logs retained 6 months. Accessible only to facilitator and research team. Student identifiers anonymised in aggregate reports. Data per KVKK and GDPR. Students informed via course syllabus. Data destruction certificate at academic year end.
realizations.proof: The deployed prototype does not use a backend server or external database. Leaderboard data is stored locally in the player’s browser through localStorage. No login or account system is required. The full formal log retention process described in the proposal, including facilitator-only access and destruction certificate, is not implemented in the prototype.

realizations.label: MDA Analysis
realizations.promised: mechanics: Puzzle chain (sequential sorting challenges), Algorithm selection panel, Step-by-step execution validation, Hint token system (3 per session), Sandbox mode (unscored experimentation), Leaderboard by difficulty level reached, Speed-sort challenge mode. · dynamics: The step-by-step execution validation mechanic forces students to think at the algorithm's execution level rather than just knowing the algorithm name, producing deeper cognitive engagement. Hint token scarcity creates authentic cost-benefit reasoning about knowledge confidence. Sandbox mode provides a low-stakes exploration space that complements the scored puzzle chain. · aesthetics: Challenge (each puzzle requires genuine algorithm application, not guessing), Discovery (sandbox mode enables free algorithm experimentation), Accomplishment (advanced difficulty puzzle completion signals genuine SEN4012 mastery), Submission (puzzle aesthetic creates engaging spatial representation of abstract sorting concepts).

realizations.label: Mechanics
realizations.promised: pts · lb · progress · challenge
realizations.proof: The proposed mechanics are implemented. Points are represented through the score system, leaderboard records player results, progress is shown through levels and completion states, and challenge is created through algorithm-specific puzzle rules and invalid move feedback.

realizations.label: SDT — Autonomy support
realizations.promised: Students choose their approach to each sorting puzzle independently — selecting which sorting algorithm to apply, determining step sequence, and deciding when to use hint tokens versus reasoning independently. The open sandbox mode allows students to freely experiment with algorithm variants without scoring pressure, supporting self-directed algorithm exploration beyond the structured puzzle chain.
realizations.proof: The deployed game supports autonomy by allowing players to choose the algorithm mode, difficulty level, hint usage, reset/new puzzle actions, daily challenge, and custom arrays through the Level Editor. However, the full sandbox mode for experimenting with algorithm variants without scoring pressure is not separately implemented.

realizations.label: SDT — Competence support
realizations.promised: Each puzzle completion with a correct sorting algorithm application awards XP proportional to the solution's time complexity optimality, making algorithm knowledge directly rewarding in a quantifiable way. The puzzle difficulty ladder (Bubble Sort fundamentals to Heap Sort) provides an explicit, topic-aligned competence staircase. Incorrect solutions reveal the execution step where the chosen algorithm diverged from correct behaviour.
realizations.proof: The deployed game strongly supports competence. Players receive immediate feedback for valid and invalid moves, see explanations when a move violates the algorithm rule, and track their performance through score, moves, time, hints used, and star rating. The level system also creates a visible difficulty ladder from easier to harder puzzles.

realizations.label: SDT — Relatedness support
realizations.promised: Post-session puzzle board shared with the class shows which students reached which difficulty levels, creating peer conversation about sorting algorithm trade-offs. Group speed-sort challenges (simultaneous puzzle solving with live class comparison) create shared competitive energy. Pair mode allows two students to collaboratively solve a sorting challenge, requiring negotiated algorithm selection.
realizations.proof: The deployed prototype partially supports relatedness through the local leaderboard, Daily Challenge, and shareable puzzle links. These features allow comparison and peer challenge in a limited form. However, real class-wide online leaderboard, pair mode, and simultaneous group speed-sort challenges are not implemented.

realizations.label: Octalysis (0.0-1.0 per drive)
realizations.proof: A formal Octalysis score or white-hat/black-hat analysis was not included in the imported proposal and was not implemented as a separate measurement in the prototype. The game still uses motivational elements such as challenge, progress, feedback, achievement, and leaderboard.

However, the full Entropy Demon and Algorithm Keeper story is not deeply integrated into gameplay text or progression.

realizations.label: Game Overview
realizations.promised: A tile-puzzle visualiser for SEN4012 where students physically sort array tiles using a chosen algorithm — each move costs XP proportional to its operation count.
realizations.proof: The deployed prototype matches the proposed game overview. It is a tile-puzzle algorithm visualiser where students interact with algorithm challenges, follow algorithm-specific rules, receive feedback, and are evaluated through moves, time, score, hints, and star rating.

realizations.label: Concept-Game Mappings
realizations.promised: concept: Sorting algorithm execution rules, mechanic: Validation function rejects any move violating algorithm rules, dynamic: MergeSort: cannot skip merge step; left pointer must advance monotonically, aesthetic: Challenge + Mastery, rationale: Constraint-enforcement turns the puzzle from tile-sort into algorithm-execution: student is constrained to think and act like the algorithm itself · concept: Algorithm efficiency (operation count), mechanic: Move counter; star rating penalises excess moves, dynamic: BubbleSort min 28 moves on 8 tiles; 40 moves = star drops, aesthetic: Mastery + Achievement, rationale: Move counter makes efficiency visceral — fewer moves feels better, which is the intuition behind optimisation · concept: Pivot selection in QuickSort, mechanic: Tap tile to declare pivot; board auto-colours red (>) blue (<); partition follows, dynamic: Worst-case pivot (always max) → maximally unbalanced → more moves → star drop, aesthetic: Discovery, rationale: Visual partition immediately shows why random/median-of-3 strategies outperform worst-case picks

Efficiency is represented through moves, score, time, hints, and stars. Quick Sort, Merge Sort, Heap Sort, Binary Search, and BFS concepts are included as playable modes. However, some advanced mappings, such as fully animated partition/merge visualisations and detailed complexity trade-off panels, are simplified in the prototype.

These values can support completion rate and playability evaluation. However, the full Kirkpatrick KPI process, including post-session survey, pre/post chapter quiz improvement, lab assignment comparison, and final exam performance analysis, is not implemented inside the game.

realizations.label: Tusler Risks
realizations.hint: ≥4 risks across all 4 categories; ≥25-char mitigation each
realizations.promised: risk: Technical Platform Failure During Session, category: Tiger, prob: High, impact: High, mitigation: Conduct full platform test on target lab devices 24h before session. Prepare a paper-based fallback version of the sorting puzzle game challenges. Verify browser compatibility on all student devices., responsive: Switch to prepared paper-based fallback immediately. Instructor verbally presents SEN4012 algorithm challenges; students answer on printed worksheets. Session data manually entered into system post-session. · risk: Widespread Rule Misunderstanding at Session Start, category: Tiger, prob: High, impact: High, mitigation: Provide multi-modal game introduction: verbal briefing, printed rule card, and in-game tutorial overlay for the sorting puzzle game. Conduct a 2-minute practice round before official scoring begins., responsive: Facilitator pauses game immediately on detecting widespread confusion. Re-delivers rule explanation using a specific worked example. Q&A window opened before resuming. Game timer reset if necessary. · risk: Overemphasis on Winning Over Learning, category: Puppy, prob: High, impact: Low, mitigation: Design sorting puzzle game scoring to reward both performance and SEN4012 algorithm concept demonstration. Integrate mandatory reflection checkpoints mid-session where students must explain their decision rationale., responsive: Instructor intervenes to redirect student focus to SEN4012 algorithm learning objectives. Post-session assessment weights conceptual understanding over game performance score explicitly. · risk: Student Disengagement After Early Failure, category: Tiger, prob: High, impact: Medium, mitigation: Implement adaptive difficulty that reduces challenge intensity if a student is failing repeatedly in the sorting puzzle game. Provide visible progress indicators so students always see incremental advancement., responsive: Facilitator provides individual encouragement and targeted hint. Adaptive system reduces challenge difficulty for the next SEN4012 algorithm task. Optional easier entry point offered without public announcement. · risk: Assessment Misalignment with Course Learning Outcomes, category: Alligator, prob: Low, impact: High, mitigation: Conduct formal SEN4012 algorithm curriculum alignment review before deployment. Map every sorting puzzle game challenge item to specific course learning outcome statements in the alignment matrix., responsive: Instructor conducts post-session targeted debrief correcting any SEN4012 algorithm misconceptions surfaced by game challenge outcomes. Misaligned challenge items flagged for removal before next session. · risk: Incorrect Big-O Reasoning Reinforced as Correct, category: Alligator, prob: Low, impact: High, mitigation: Embed automated Big-O correctness checking in the sorting puzzle game — system must validate algorithm classification against verified answer key before awarding XP. Manual review of edge cases by instructor before deployment., responsive: Instructor delivers targeted post-session debrief correcting identified Big-O misconceptions. Specific algorithm challenge items producing widespread incorrect answers flagged for redesign before next session. · risk: Step-Level Validation Feels Punitive Rather Than Educational, category: Tiger, prob: High, impact: High, mitigation: Design validation feedback as positive guidance rather than binary fail: 'This step is valid but consider whether this is the most efficient next move.' Animations reinforce correct steps before flagging errors., responsive: Instructor reframes error messages in session debrief as diagnostic information, not punishment. Post-session error pattern analysis shared with students as a personalised learning roadmap for next session. · risk: Sandbox Mode Time Consumption Leaving Insufficient Session Time, category: Tiger, prob: High, impact: Medium, mitigation: Enforce a maximum sandbox time allocation of 8 minutes per session, with a visible countdown and automatic transition to scored puzzle chain when the allocation expires. Sandbox findings prompts students to apply to scored chain., responsive: Facilitator announces sandbox time limit 2 minutes before expiry. Students who have not transitioned receive a facilitator-directed starter puzzle aligned to their sandbox exploration to bridge the transition. · risk: Boss Puzzle Algorithm Selection Produces Single Correct Answer Perception, category: Puppy, prob: High, impact: Low, mitigation: Design Boss Puzzle with two demonstrably valid algorithm solutions at different complexity-trade-off points. Award full XP for both, but differentiate feedback to highlight the performance difference between the two correct approaches., responsive: Instructor uses Boss Puzzle multiple-valid-solution structure in debrief to teach algorithm selection reasoning beyond binary correctness — reinforcing that SEN4012 algorithm selection is context-dependent, not prescriptive.
realizations.proof: The deployed prototype addresses several technical and usability risks through a browser-based implementation, GitHub Pages deployment, clear How to Play instructions, immediate feedback for invalid moves, reset/new puzzle options, responsive design, and a simple local leaderboard. However, the complete risk plan described in the proposal, including paper-based fallback materials, formal instructor debrief process, adaptive difficulty engine, and manual review workflow, is not fully implemented inside the game.

realizations.label: Why (Motivation)
realizations.promised: Students are motivated through three SDT core drives: Autonomy — puzzle approach selection and sandbox exploration give students genuine control over their algorithm learning strategy, respecting that different students prefer different entry points into algorithm understanding. Competence — step-by-step execution validation provides far more precise feedback than answer-level checking, making algorithm understanding feel like a real, verifiable skill rather than an arbitrary test performance. Relatedness — group speed-sort challenges and pair mode transform individual algorithm puzzle-solving into shared class events with authentic peer engagement. Through the Octalysis lens: Challenge, Development & Accomplishment, Discovery, Social Influence.
realizations.proof: The deployed game partially implements the proposed motivation design. Autonomy is supported through mode, level, hint, reset, new puzzle, Daily Challenge, and Level Editor choices. Competence is supported through immediate feedback, scoring, timer, stars, and invalid move explanations. Relatedness is partially supported through local leaderboard and share links. However, sandbox mode, pair mode, and full class-wide speed-sort events are not implemented.

realizations.label: How (Ethics)
realizations.promised: Student participation is voluntary; equivalent written task available for opt-outs. All performance data anonymised in aggregate reports. Data collected under KVKK and GDPR, disclosed in course syllabus. Assessment based on participation and reflection, not competitive ranking. Instructor monitors emotional wellbeing; distress triggers private check-in.
realizations.proof: The deployed prototype follows a low-data approach: it does not require login, account creation, backend storage, or external database usage. Leaderboard data is stored locally in the player’s browser. However, the full formal ethics process described in the proposal, including opt-out written task, official KVKK/GDPR disclosure workflow, and instructor wellbeing monitoring, is not implemented inside the game.

projectEvidence.notes: SortQuest is deployed as a browser-based HTML/CSS/JavaScript game on GitHub Pages. The current version includes 8 algorithm modes, 5 difficulty levels, scoring, timer, hints, star rating, level editor, share link generation, daily challenge, and browser-based leaderboard. No login or installation is required.

deployment.platform: Any modern browser on desktop or laptop. Recommended: Chrome, Edge, Firefox, or Safari. Internet connection is required only to open the GitHub Pages link.
deployment.description: A tile-puzzle visualiser for SEN4012 where students physically sort array tiles using a chosen algorithm — each move costs XP proportional to its operation count.
deployment.audience: SEN4012 Analysis of Algorithms students (3rd/4th year, n≈35)
deployment.howToPlay: Open the play link in a modern browser. Select an algorithm mode and a difficulty level. Solve the puzzle by selecting or moving tiles according to the selected algorithm’s rules. Valid moves are accepted and invalid moves are rejected with an explanation. The game tracks moves, time, score, hints used, and final star rating. Players can use Hint, Reset, and New Puzzle buttons. They can also try the Daily Challenge, load a custom array in Level Editor, generate a share link, and compare scores on the leaderboard.
deployment.accessRights: Open to all players. No login, password, installation, or account is required. The game runs directly in the browser.

sixDim.what: 8 puzzle modes (Sequential Sort, Binary Search, Insertion Sort, MergeSort, QuickSort, HeapSort, DP Knapsack, BFS Graph); drag-and-drop tile engine with algorithm-rule validation; star rating (★★★ = min moves + under par time); hint system (−1 star); Daily Sort Challenge (global seed, leaderboard); Level Editor (shareable URL)
sixDim.why: Flow: 4 tiles (level 1) → 64 tiles (level 5). SDT Competence: visible improvement. MDA: Mechanics = constrained drag-drop; Dynamics = optimal move discovery; Aesthetic = Mastery + Sensation (satisfying merge animation).
sixDim.who: Free Spirits 40% (experiment, try to break algorithm), Achievers 40% (3-star perfection, Daily leaderboard), Explorers 20% (edge cases in QuickSort pivot)
sixDim.when: Modes unlock sequentially with chapters. Daily Challenge always available. Level Editor from week 2.
sixDim.how: Wrong moves rejected immediately with algorithm-specific explanation ("This violates MergeSort rule: left pointer must advance monotonically"). No ambiguity. 0 false accepts by validation function.

state.narrativeTheme: The SortQuest realm is in chaos — the Library of Ordered Knowledge has been scrambled by the Entropy Demon. As the last Algorithm Keeper, restore order using the ancient sorting laws.
state.characters:
Algorithm Engineer (Student): Selects and deploys correct algorithms to clear each complexity wave using knowledge and pattern recognition.
Complexity Horde (NPC Enemy): Waves of algorithmic problems with specific complexity classes requiring matched algorithm types.
Instructor Avatar (NPC Guide): In-game professor providing contextual hints, chapter intros, and debrief prompts.
state.storyWorld: The Sorting Academy is an ancient institution where computational knowledge is tested not by memorisation but by execution. Six halls represent the six sorting algorithm families: the Bubble Hall, the Selection Chamber, the Insertion Corridor, the Merge Sanctum, the Quick Arena, and the Heap Pinnacle. Each hall tests a specific algorithm family with increasing input complexity and edge-case difficulty. The Academy Boss Puzzle chamber sits at the pinnacle — accessible only to students who have demonstrated mastery across all six halls.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Validation function rejects any move violating algorithm rules
conceptMappings.dynamic: MergeSort: cannot skip merge step; left pointer must advance monotonically
conceptMappings.rationale: Constraint-enforcement turns the puzzle from tile-sort into algorithm-execution: student is constrained to think and act like the algorithm itself

conceptMappings.mechanic: Move counter; star rating penalises excess moves
conceptMappings.dynamic: BubbleSort min 28 moves on 8 tiles; 40 moves = star drops
conceptMappings.rationale: Move counter makes efficiency visceral — fewer moves feels better, which is the intuition behind optimisation

conceptMappings.mechanic: Tap tile to declare pivot; board auto-colours red (>) blue (<); partition follows
conceptMappings.dynamic: Worst-case pivot (always max) → maximally unbalanced → more moves → star drop
conceptMappings.rationale: Visual partition immediately shows why random/median-of-3 strategies outperform worst-case picks

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Puzzle chain (sequential sorting challenges), Algorithm selection panel, Step-by-step execution validation, Hint token system (3 per session), Sandbox mode (unscored experimentation), Leaderboard by difficulty level reached, Speed-sort challenge mode.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: The step-by-step execution validation mechanic forces students to think at the algorithm's execution level rather than just knowing the algorithm name, producing deeper cognitive engagement. Hint token scarcity creates authentic cost-benefit reasoning about knowledge confidence. Sandbox mode provides a low-stakes exploration space that complements the scored puzzle chain.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Challenge (each puzzle requires genuine algorithm application, not guessing), Discovery (sandbox mode enables free algorithm experimentation), Accomplishment (advanced difficulty puzzle completion signals genuine SEN4012 mastery), Submission (puzzle aesthetic creates engaging spatial representation of abstract sorting concepts).

state.introText: Welcome to the Sorting Academy. You are an algorithm apprentice, and your task is simple: sort the data correctly, step by step, using the algorithm the puzzle demands. Choose the wrong algorithm and the step-level validator will show exactly where your execution diverged from the correct trace — no vague wrong answers here. Use your hint tokens wisely; you have only three per session. Complete each sorting hall to advance and reach the Boss Puzzle chamber where the full sorting hierarchy is tested in a single complex trial. The Academy is waiting.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct full platform test on target lab devices 24h before session. Prepare a paper-based fallback version of the sorting puzzle game challenges. Verify browser compatibility on all student devices.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Switch to prepared paper-based fallback immediately. Instructor verbally presents SEN4012 algorithm challenges; students answer on printed worksheets. Session data manually entered into system post-session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Widespread Rule Misunderstanding at Session Start
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Provide multi-modal game introduction: verbal briefing, printed rule card, and in-game tutorial overlay for the sorting puzzle game. Conduct a 2-minute practice round before official scoring begins.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator pauses game immediately on detecting widespread confusion. Re-delivers rule explanation using a specific worked example. Q&A window opened before resuming. Game timer reset if necessary.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design sorting puzzle game scoring to reward both performance and SEN4012 algorithm concept demonstration. Integrate mandatory reflection checkpoints mid-session where students must explain their decision rationale.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor intervenes to redirect student focus to SEN4012 algorithm learning objectives. Post-session assessment weights conceptual understanding over game performance score explicitly.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Implement adaptive difficulty that reduces challenge intensity if a student is failing repeatedly in the sorting puzzle game. Provide visible progress indicators so students always see incremental advancement.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator provides individual encouragement and targeted hint. Adaptive system reduces challenge difficulty for the next SEN4012 algorithm task. Optional easier entry point offered without public announcement.

tuslerRisks.risk: Assessment Misalignment with Course Learning Outcomes
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct formal SEN4012 algorithm curriculum alignment review before deployment. Map every sorting puzzle game challenge item to specific course learning outcome statements in the alignment matrix.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor conducts post-session targeted debrief correcting any SEN4012 algorithm misconceptions surfaced by game challenge outcomes. Misaligned challenge items flagged for removal before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Incorrect Big-O Reasoning Reinforced as Correct
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Embed automated Big-O correctness checking in the sorting puzzle game — system must validate algorithm classification against verified answer key before awarding XP. Manual review of edge cases by instructor before deployment.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor delivers targeted post-session debrief correcting identified Big-O misconceptions. Specific algorithm challenge items producing widespread incorrect answers flagged for redesign before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Step-Level Validation Feels Punitive Rather Than Educational
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design validation feedback as positive guidance rather than binary fail: 'This step is valid but consider whether this is the most efficient next move.' Animations reinforce correct steps before flagging errors.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor reframes error messages in session debrief as diagnostic information, not punishment. Post-session error pattern analysis shared with students as a personalised learning roadmap for next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Sandbox Mode Time Consumption Leaving Insufficient Session Time
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Enforce a maximum sandbox time allocation of 8 minutes per session, with a visible countdown and automatic transition to scored puzzle chain when the allocation expires. Sandbox findings prompts students to apply to scored chain.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator announces sandbox time limit 2 minutes before expiry. Students who have not transitioned receive a facilitator-directed starter puzzle aligned to their sandbox exploration to bridge the transition.

tuslerRisks.risk: Boss Puzzle Algorithm Selection Produces Single Correct Answer Perception
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design Boss Puzzle with two demonstrably valid algorithm solutions at different complexity-trade-off points. Award full XP for both, but differentiate feedback to highlight the performance difference between the two correct approaches.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor uses Boss Puzzle multiple-valid-solution structure in debrief to teach algorithm selection reasoning beyond binary correctness — reinforcing that SEN4012 algorithm selection is context-dependent, not prescriptive.

state.gameOverview: A tile-puzzle visualiser for SEN4012 where students physically sort array tiles using a chosen algorithm — each move costs XP proportional to its operation count.
state.deliverableJust: A browser-based tile puzzle is uniquely suited because: (1) Active decision-making with immediate consequence creates stronger encoding than passive delivery; (2) Game mechanics directly parallel domain processes (see Concept-to-Game Mapping); (3) Performance logging enables objective Bloom L3+ skill assessment.
state.sdtAutonomy: Students choose their approach to each sorting puzzle independently — selecting which sorting algorithm to apply, determining step sequence, and deciding when to use hint tokens versus reasoning independently. The open sandbox mode allows students to freely experiment with algorithm variants without scoring pressure, supporting self-directed algorithm exploration beyond the structured puzzle chain.
state.sdtCompetence: Each puzzle completion with a correct sorting algorithm application awards XP proportional to the solution's time complexity optimality, making algorithm knowledge directly rewarding in a quantifiable way. The puzzle difficulty ladder (Bubble Sort fundamentals to Heap Sort) provides an explicit, topic-aligned competence staircase. Incorrect solutions reveal the execution step where the chosen algorithm diverged from correct behaviour.
state.sdtRelatedness: Post-session puzzle board shared with the class shows which students reached which difficulty levels, creating peer conversation about sorting algorithm trade-offs. Group speed-sort challenges (simultaneous puzzle solving with live class comparison) create shared competitive energy. Pair mode allows two students to collaboratively solve a sorting challenge, requiring negotiated algorithm selection.
state.platform: Browser-based web application; any modern browser. No installation required. Compatible with IKU computer labs, student devices, and mobile browsers.
state.narrativeEvolution:
Hall 1-2 — Bubble and Selection Halls (O(n²) Fundamentals): Apprentice completes Bubble Sort and Selection Sort puzzles at increasing input sizes. Step-level validation reveals common O(n²) execution misconceptions early. Hint tokens tempting but advised to save. SEN4012 concept: O(n²) sorting algorithm execution patterns and comparison count analysis.

Hall 3-4 — Insertion and Merge Halls (Transition to O(n log n)): Insertion Sort puzzles test best-case awareness. Merge Sort introduces divide-and-conquer execution tracing — the first multi-step recursive structure. Complexity jumps; some students use first hint tokens here. SEN4012 concept: insertion sort best-case O(n) and merge sort O(n log n) divide-and-conquer execution.

Hall 5-6 — QuickSort and Heap Halls (O(n log n) Mastery): Pivot selection in QuickSort puzzles tests worst-case awareness. HeapSort introduces heap property validation as a pre-sort step. Advanced students preserve hint tokens; others spend them here. SEN4012 concept: QuickSort pivot strategy, worst-case O(n²) scenario, and HeapSort O(n log n) guarantee.

Boss Puzzle — Mixed-Requirement Trial: The Academy presents a complex scenario with multiple data sets, each requiring a different optimal sorting algorithm. Correct algorithm selection for each data set determines the final quality score. Debrief maps every step-level decision to SEN4012 sorting algorithm selection criteria and comparative analysis.
state.logPrivacy: Activity logs retained 6 months. Accessible only to facilitator and research team. Student identifiers anonymised in aggregate reports. Data per KVKK and GDPR. Students informed via course syllabus. Data destruction certificate at academic year end.
state.howEthics: Student participation is voluntary; equivalent written task available for opt-outs. All performance data anonymised in aggregate reports. Data collected under KVKK and GDPR, disclosed in course syllabus. Assessment based on participation and reflection, not competitive ranking. Instructor monitors emotional wellbeing; distress triggers private check-in.
state.whyMotivation: Students are motivated through three SDT core drives: Autonomy — puzzle approach selection and sandbox exploration give students genuine control over their algorithm learning strategy, respecting that different students prefer different entry points into algorithm understanding. Competence — step-by-step execution validation provides far more precise feedback than answer-level checking, making algorithm understanding feel like a real, verifiable skill rather than an arbitrary test performance. Relatedness — group speed-sort challenges and pair mode transform individual algorithm puzzle-solving into shared class events with authentic peer engagement. Through the Octalysis lens: Challenge, Development & Accomplishment, Discovery, Social Influence.

gameDynamics.progressionSystems: Puzzle chain advances through six sorting algorithm families in complexity order: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Merge Sort, QuickSort, and Heap Sort. Each family contains 3 puzzles of increasing input size and edge-case complexity. Completing a family unlocks the next and awards a family badge. The final boss puzzle requires choosing and applying the optimal algorithm for a complex mixed-requirement scenario.
gameDynamics.feedbackLoops: Step-validation feedback highlights the exact execution step where a student's algorithm trace diverges from correct behaviour, providing precise actionable feedback. Hint token use is tracked and displayed in the post-session report, providing feedback on knowledge confidence patterns. Sandbox mode provides self-directed feedback through observable algorithm execution without scoring pressure.
gameDynamics.competition: Leaderboard by puzzle chain depth and efficiency (hints used per puzzle). Weekly Sort Master badge for the student with the deepest chain and fewest hints used. Speed-sort challenge mode (all students attempt the same puzzle simultaneously with live class timer) creates high-energy competitive events throughout the semester.
gameDynamics.collaboration: Pair mode pairs students on a shared puzzle: one selects the algorithm, one executes the steps. Role alternation per puzzle ensures both students engage both selection and execution competencies. Disagreements on algorithm selection trigger a collaborative reasoning phase before commitment to the solution path.
gameDynamics.adaptability: Puzzle difficulty and input complexity are configurable per session. Hint token allocation is adjustable for assessment vs practice contexts. Sandbox mode can be enabled or disabled per session. Boss puzzle can be swapped per session to cover different algorithm category combinations. Speed-sort challenge timing is adjustable.

state.personaList:
Persona 1 — The Precision Solver: Approaches each puzzle with full algorithm analysis before beginning. Rarely uses hints. Motivated by completing the boss puzzle with zero hints.

Persona 2 — The Sandbox Explorer: Prefers unscored sandbox mode for algorithm experimentation before attempting scored puzzles. Uses sandbox to test incorrect approaches safely before committing.

Persona 3 — The Speed Runner: Prioritises puzzle completion speed over optimality. May select a correct but suboptimal sorting algorithm for large inputs to avoid decision time cost. Needs instructor prompts to reconsider efficiency.

socialInteraction.collaboration: Pair mode assigns algorithm selection to one student and execution trace to the other — creating complementary collaboration that ensures both students engage both cognitive layers of sorting algorithm mastery.
socialInteraction.competition: Class speed-sort challenge mode creates simultaneous competitive puzzle solving visible to all students — turning individual algorithm practice into a shared competitive performance event.
socialInteraction.communityBuilding: Post-session class puzzle board showing depth reached and hints used creates transparent peer comparison that drives improvement across sessions; sandbox findings are shared in class debrief.

conceptualMap.description: Step validation forces students to trace algorithm execution precisely — developing execution-level understanding that distinguishes genuine algorithm knowledge from name recognition.

conceptualMap.description: Boss puzzle presents scenarios requiring selection of the optimal algorithm across multiple competing criteria — operationalising the SEN4012 algorithm selection decision framework.
Sandbox mode operationalises algorithmic hypothesis testing — students can experiment with algorithm variants on test inputs without scoring pressure, developing genuine algorithm intuition.

Algorithm selection panel presents competing sorting options with different complexity profiles — requiring students to reason about best-case, worst-case, and average-case performance simultaneously.
